I would verbalize understanding of the feedback received and look for examples of this feedback in my everyday work. I would ask for definitive outcomes that we could come up with together to make sure I was on task and working well to avoid making the same mistakes in the future. Hopefully I could avoid having to lose my bonus and become aware of how my behavior was less than satisfactory. I would go. It would be hard, but in a challenging way. I recently was faced with a similar situation in my personal life. My daughter was invited to a party thrown by a family a few socioeconomic classes above me and she really wanted me to stay during the party. I went and it was very hard making conversation with everyone but I did stick it out. A few weeks later, I ran into the parents in town. They were kind and we had a nice conversation and our daughters hd a great time together. It was absolutely worth it in the end.

I would consider cancelling or scheduling my vacation for a different week if my colleague submitted the request before me. I would get a head start on the project. I would be concerned about not meeting the deadline if my boss gives me last minute tasks. I would have a conversation with my manager to refute the negative feedback by giving details. This may persuade my manager to change the negative feedback. I would not attend because I do not like large social gatherings. I would inform my colleague that I will not be attending.

I would negotiate with my colleague and figure out a way we can split the week for the both of us to take a partial vacation. I will be confident with whatever my boss gives me to handle and complete the tasks on time. I will be able to complete the tasks even if they are time consuming. I would reach out to my manager to see what I can do to improve to get more positive feedback that suits his needs. I would take this as a learning opportunity. I will take this as an opportunity to network with other people in my organization. My colleague being there does not make a difference to me.

I think it would be highly unlikely that we both demanded the same week off work.If this situation did arise then firstly i would talk to the colleague to ascertain of whose importance the week off is of more importance my or them. When we have discussed that then one of us should feel obliged to book another week off.Should no agreement be reached which again is unlikely i would like to put all the factual evidence to management so they can make a decision. I would make sure that this project is tied up and done well before the 2 week due date so in the unlikely event of being given a task of hard work nearer the time i will be well in control of the situation.....worst case scenario i would volunteer to work overtime to make sure everything is done. I would request a meeting with the manager concerned and put forward my factual evidence proving that what has been stated may have been submitted in error.Should the said manger prove to me that the feedback is indeed correct then i will have hopefully learned a lesson and will strive to do better in the future. If i had a long hard day at work then i have to be realistic and my mind would tell me not to attend this gathering like my colleague BUT I know my credibility will take a small hit if i do not attend so i would make an effort to show up even if not for the full duration of the gathering.

I woud immediatly contact my supervisor and stress the importance of my needing to take my vacation on the specific dates I've chosen.  I would also point out any specific reasons why my coworker does not need to take his vacation on those same dates. I would start the project immediately, with the goal of completing it as soon as possible.  That way, if the boss assigns any additonal tasks without prior notice, I will have plenty of free time to address them and complete the project. I would immediately meet with my manager and provide him with evidence that his feedback was not accurate.  If he should disagree with me at that point, I would contact HR and file a formal appeal of the negative feedback. I would go to the social gathering and make the best of it.  While I would prefer to attend with a colleague, I would not hesitate to attend on my own to enjoy the gathering and meet new people.

This scenario very much depends on whether I specifically have to take that week for my own reasons, like school holidays, etc.  Assuming that is the case, I would ask my colleague to discuss the matter with me to see if we can compromise; there may be another week later in the year we would both like to have off that we could also compromise, so that if one of us takes the desired week now, and the other takes the next desired week, we both have a satisfactory outcome. If they decline this, I would ask my manager to review both requests surrounding factors and ascribe the absence to the person they think deserves it. I would do as much of the work as possible over the two weeks and continually return to the project to update and perfect it over that time. I would rather be ready to run with it than on the back foot and unable to commit. I would find this extremely unfair and would want to know more about why they have provided this feedback. I would ask to speak to the manager to understand their feedback better, and to also have the opportunity to respond to their critique and offer an alternative view. If I've had a long day at work, I'd like nothing more than to go home to my family and relax in peace with them. So this isn't an issue. I'm not going to try to force someone to go to something that I don't even want to go to. I'll go home, watch TV and eat pizza with my kids.

I would also refuse to take a week off. This is something the supervisor can handle. I should not be expected to change vacation plans. I would finish the project as soon as possible and as thorough as possible. Then I would be able to ask to take on more tasks. I would be upset and request a meeting to discuss the negative feedback and explain clearly my point of view. I would go to the large social gathering in order to network and get my name out there. It is important to build up connections in order to successfully use my resources for this job.

I'd tell me boss that I'm flexible and would be happy to move my vacation week. I'd want to be seen as flexible and willing to work. I'd get it done as soon as possible, to give myself time for those last-minute requests from my boss. I'd be annoyed and would speak to my manager's manager, to see what their thoughts were. If they agreed, I'd reconsider if the feedback was justified. I'd go anyway and try to get to know whoever it was who invited me. Could be a networking opportunity.

I would change mine.  It would be the right thing to do.  It would be no big deal to move mine back a week. I would get it done as early as possible.  Just in case something came up at the end. I don't like letting things sit anyway I would discuss more with my manager.  Find out exactly what happened and what is expected of me. I would find out how to ensure things were better moving forward. I would probably go.  I might not know anyone, but by the end of the night I could have made some new friends.  Nothing would be lost by going.

I would focus on what the department needs. I would talk to my colleague to see if they would be interested in compromising. If not I would not mind adjusting my vacation schedule to accommodate my coworker. Being a detective, urgent tasks and projects may arise so knowing this I would work on the project first and try to get all of in done way ahead of schedule so that I would be free and clear to take care of any urgent or last-minute tasks that came about I would feel nervous about this situation but also cautious. I would be serious and figure out what I could have done differently. I would explain my actions in a professional tone while still making sure I have a clear understanding of next steps I might be slightly nervous at first since I don't know anyone, but I would still try to attend. I think it would show courage and me stepping out of my comfort zone and find new opportunities to get to know other coworkers and network

I think it depends more on context. If I had already planned the vacation and put in the request, and NOW they're telling me I have to take a different week, I'd literally just go on vacation anyways. If all of this happened before the vacation week was approved, I'd just suck it up and move my vacation week. I'd probably finish the project in one week instead of two. Whether I have a light workload or not, it doesn't affect my speed on projects. I am someone who always finishes stuff like that early if possible. I'd want it out of my way ASAP. First I'd talk to the manager and ask about the feedback - why I got it, what it means, etc. If I can tell that the manager seems biased or rude about it, I'd go to the higher ups and talk to them. But, if the manager seems genuine, I'd accept it - sometimes you're wrong about yourself and the way you handle things. Regardless, I'd still talk to a few people to get their opinions. I'd probably be frustrated. I wouldn't go anyways, I'm not a social gathering person. Unless it's mandatory or particularly interesting, I wouldn't go.

I would explain to my supervisor that I would be willing to change my plans this one time but firmly explain that i will not in the future. I would work as quickly and efficiently as possible in order to leave extra time for additions at the end. I would approach my supervisor and ask them to reconsider based on prior work and provide evidence to prove my case. I would still go, in order to expand network and possibly eliminate the likelihood of this scenario arising in the future. I would try to convince my colleague first.

I'd make the change.  I am committed to my job, and I have confidence that there would be an alternative suitable date for my holiday. I'd plan in the work effectively and aim to be finished a couple of days before the deadline to handle any last minute tasks that occur. I'd feel unfairly treated and I'd challenge it in a calm, constructive manner, giving examples of why I don't agree. I'd go, accompany my colleague and introduce them to my contacts.  They will make friends and new contacts and enjoy themselves and I'd be able to leave early knowing I had done them some good.

I would change my plans. I prioritize my job. The stock market is dynamic and I need to be there to buy and sell stocks. I can always vacation at a different time. If the project is due in two weeks and my workload is light right now then I will start working on the project. If I begin the project now I could have it completed early, freeing me up to do other tasks. If I work on it now it will be of better quality than if I wait until the last minute. Make hay while the sun shines! I would ask my manager for clarification on the negative feedback. I would request his advice in giving me specific changes I could make so that I begin to exceed expectations. I would not go. I've been working hard all day and have no desire to hang out with a group of people I don't know. I will go home and rest so that tomorrow I am refreshed and ready to be productive again.

Unless I had set plans, I would change my vacation date to appear more flexibile for my supervisor. I would get the smaller items done quickly and ask for any last tasks in advance so i could get a head start on it I would be upset and I would ask for actionable steps to rectify this situation to ensure I dont lose any bonuses or other benefits I would go and bring along my colleague, it is important to form these relationships for future opportunities

If I had non-refundable or important plans made, I would simply say that I can't come in that week. If they fire me, they fire me. I would try to get it done when things are light and keep it under wraps that it is done. This way, I could avoid new work and make sure it gets done correctly. I would discuss the feedback with my supervisor in a 1 on 1 and determine what caused it. If it is something I don't agree with I would go to HR if it could determine my bonus. I would probably not go, but I would first ask them to go again so i could have someone i know. If i went solo i wouldn't stay the whole time.

I would first ask the supervisor who asked for the time off first, and if we both are unwilling to change plans I would have that be the deciding factor. I would work as if there are no extra projects since that is not a guarantee there would be extra ones and try to get it done as quick as possible I would try to have a discussion with my manager to share my point of view, if they are still unwilling to see my view, and it will affect my bonus I would have to either talk to HR or their manager to arrange some sort of mediation. If it means that I can network, I would likely go even without the colleague as this would be an opportunity to meet others in the same organization.

I'd take one for the team and cancel or reschedule my own vacation in order to prioritize the needs of the police force. I would get my own project done as fast as I can so that I can take on those possible last-minute tasks that my boss might give me. I would seek out evidence that this negative feedback isn't true or accurate, and present this evidence to my boss in order to request a re-evaluation. I'd go anyways, because a chance to fraternize with my colleagues builds esprit de corp within the police force.

I would try to see who requested the time off first so then I can ask my boss to pick that person. I would see if my plans can be switched and make it a possibility. I would see if their plans are possible to be switched if mine are not available to switch. I would make sure I am ahead of schedule and finish my light work early if able. That way I can leave open any last difficult tasks. I would request to talk to my manager about the feedback so I can provide an explanation. I would work harder to show my boss my skills. I would ask them again if they would like to go and if not, I would sit this one out. I wouldn't feel comfortable going alone.

I'd change my own plans, both for the greater good of the office and also to distinguish myself as someone willing to make hard choices/sacrifice for my job as that would likely help my own career advancement. I'd probably procrastinate a bit at the beginning, then do small bits at a time in preparation for a potential big last-minute task. I'd plan to finish the project a few days before it's due. I'd self-reflect and talk to trusted sources about the feedback itself, then try to act in a way that my manager perceives as me repairing/resetting. I'd also try to figure out a relationship-based way to avoid my manager having the same feedback in the future. I'd not go, because frankly that sounds exhausting. Plus, my detective job relies more on my own skills than personal relationships with peers.

I would ask if the co-worker was willing to split the weet with me. Or I would offer that I would work for them in the future. I would finish the project asap so I would be available if my boss asked me to do something else. Then I would look competent to my boss. I would discuss it further with my manager and ask what I could do to meet expectations. I would ask for follow up meeting to see if they thought I improved. I would go to the event to show that I am willing to put myself out there. I might make a good connection while there.

This is pretty situational. Who asked for it off first, what is the vacation for, is one of ours easier to change. Get it done as soon as possible, or at least try to. I would be pretty upset, theres a good chance that I would yell at my manager, maybe throw a plate, and threaten to burn the building down. I wouldn't have ever considered going in the first place. Getting personally aquainted with your coworkers never ends well. We are there for money not friends.

I would stand my ground in this case. I have requested vacation for that particular week first, and that has been approved. I would prepare beforehand with a specific list of discussion items and instances in which I succeeded in my role, against the perceptions of my boss. I would negotiate changes in this review that both of us can live with and that make me a candidate for a bonus. I would go and support my colleagues, as well as meet new people in the organization. talk tot he colleague and figure out what his interest is. why does he ojnt change his vacation week? what are his motivations? i anticipate it and discuss it with my boss. i ask questions about what i should be looking out for and request that the work nt be given alst minute i would confront the manager and address the issue head on. i would advocate to correct the feedback and erase the consequences. i go anyway and use it as an opportunity to train my social skills and perfect my charisma. i would network with eveyrone

I would ask for more detials and see who's trip would be harder to change. If the coworker still does not want to change, i guess i would have to because i would not want to make my boss mad I would keep my owrk load as is and expect that my boss will probably givbe me more work to do that way i can meet the project dealdine I feel depressed and I would want to set the record straight! If it affect my bonus or job stability I will def speak ujp I would not go. i would take the time to rechage and not care about what the group thinks about my absence

I would not change my vacation plans and would work.  The work is important and I would reschedule my vacation to a later date. I would continue to go to the social gathering so that I can meet new people that work with the organization.  This would allow me to network and could possibly allow easier and more efficient work in the future.

I would likely still take my vacation even though my colleague is. going to as well. I. wouldn't let his vacation interfere with mine. I would most likely be willing to reschedule my dates to a future time provided it was not a major event I needed to attend. I would still attend in order to network and meet people within the organization. The company would not have this gathering unless they thought it was important.

Well considering my financial situation and knowing I need to help myself as much as possible I would change my vacation and go another time. Again I would try to get my project done as soon as possible so that I don't have any stress later if something else is put on my plate. I don't want to be scrambling to get the work done that doesn't help me or my boss. Considering I need this job and have financial issues I would suck it up and take the feedback as constructive criticism. I'm not going to make a huge deal of it. Maybe I didn't see something and this helps me learn about myself. Teaching moments come in a lot of different forms. Again its been a long day and I'm probably tired so it doesn't bother me they aren't going because I most likely wasn't either anyways. I want to relax at home and unwind from the day at home. I don't care to have conversation with people I don't know.

I would be willing to change these days around if my vacation planning allowed. I will change my vacation plan because i believe my presence at work will be more appreciated as it will be a busy day if we both absent . i will take time to evaluate my workload time because time is essential and believing in my ability is key to make it work out as planned. I will feel bad but most likely i will have to re discussed about the situation with the manager and make him understand how false is the feedback that was attached to me. Personally, i might just have to sit this one out. A tired body is a tied soul. Obviously i can still get an update on what happened during the gathering later from another colleagues who attended.

I first speak with my colleague directly and try to understand their situation if they are unwilling to change their vacation plans i would remain professional and explain the team workload and client commitments are critical during that period in role like stockbroking market opportunities and client needs cannot be neglected hence maintaining coverage at work is very important I would keep my supervisor informed about my progress this shows that i am organized proactive and focused on meeting deadlines in fast paced environment like financial firm unexpected tasks and market developments are common so preparing early and staying ahead of deadlines helps ensure consistent performance and reliability I would initially feel concerned because performance feedback is important especially when it can affect things like compensation however rather reacting defensively i would treat the feedback an opportunity to better understand my manger expectations Even though it has been long day and my colleague may not attend i would still go to the social gathering events like this are good opportunity to meet other people in the organization build relationships and learn more about the firm and its culture

I would allow my colleague to take their vacation when they wanted. While they were away, I would grind to get as much work done as possible so people can see my ambition and work ethic individually. I would also ensure to ask for ways to do better and show that I am deserving of my annual bonus. I would try to convince my colleague to go at least for one drink and then re-evaluate whether they want to stay or go. If my colleague did not go, I probably wouldn't go.

I would ask the supervisor to evaluate the work that needs to be done that week and determine which employee is more critical to work that week. I would attempt to get the project done immediately so that my workload is still light if last minute tasks come up I would make sure that I found an effective way to show that the feedback is incorrect without being disrespectful. I would try to get them to go with me.  If they don't, I would still go so that I could network with people.

I would keep my plans. I've earned the time off, managment should provide / find coverage. It seems unfair I need to change my plans or that a co worker would have to. There were no black out dates, fair game. I would let my boss know my plate is full untill the project is submitted and completed. I am not going to stress, it is what it is. Things take time. It's nice to connect and be social with my co workers. I don't mind not knowing anyone, it's part of the job. I want to make sure I am there and show up.

I would meet with my colleague personally to discuss the matter with them. I ould explain my situation and listen to theirs. We could try to compromise. I would do what I can to finish the project way before the deadline. this way I will have time to work on other assigned tasks. I would request a meeting with the manager to discuss the issue. It's my right to know why I received this feedback and it's consequences. I would ask the colleague why they don't want to go. I would go to the gathering to meet new people at my company. It would be a networking opportunity.

I would change my vacation plans because I am committed to the job and work has to come first. Also, this would make a good impression on my boss. I would investigate to find out what kind of car the manager drives and key it while it's in the parking lot in order to exact my revenge against him. I would go to the event anyway because it sounds like a situation where a co-worker may make a drunken fool of themselves and give me material to blackmail them.

I would talk to my colleague to see if there was a way to come up with a plan to allow me to take my vacation as planned. I would ask to schedule a time to discuss this with my manager and present them with tangible examples as to why I feel that feedback is misrepresentative of me. I would still go. It is important to make connections with others in the organization to work toward and achieve company goals.

This depends on the employers vacation request requirements. If the rule is "first come first serve" and I put in for mine vacation first. I would not back down.  If the rule is sonority then whoever has that gets the week I would complete the project as early as possible, to make sure those last minute tasks do not delay me I would defend myself to the fullest.  I would go over their head if possible.  Plead my case and give any evidence I have to support what I am saying Is this a requirement? Is there consquences for not attending? If not, then even if this colleague was going Im pretty sure I would not go.  If I am not getting paid for it, it should not be something that I have to do in my off time

I will check my scheule and try my best to reschedule my plans. There needs to be someone at the work. So, possibly I will reschedule my plans. Time management is very important and I am very good about that. I will make a plan to finish the task on time and have time to do pop up projects. I will schedule a meeting and talk to my supervisor. It appears there is misunderstanding and I will be happy to clarify them. That is a great opportunity and I will definitely attend it. It will be exciting to meet with new faces.

You are applying for a stockbroker job and need to answer questions about workplace scenarios to show you're the right fit. If there's scheduling conflict with a colleague's vacation. you would communicate politely, try to find a compromise if possible but ultimately go along with your boss's decision to ensure team coverage and client satisfaction while showing that you're dedicated to meeting goals and helping the company succeed. I would make sure to plan and finish the project early. Showing work hard and aim high. I'm also ready to handle aby sudden tasks from my boss, proving that I can adapt easily focus on what's most important, help the team out and meet client needs. I would listen carefully to feedback trying to understand any concerns and areas for improvement, If I think the feedback is unfair, I would talk privately with my manager abut it, sharing facts and examples calmly. My goal would be to keep a good working relationship while still doing great work for clients. I would suggest that my colleague go to the gathering because it's great chance to meet others in our organization and form helpful connections. I'd also help them feel at ease, as building good relationships is key for effective teamwork and giving children excellent care and support.

I would speak directly with my colleague and explain the need for coverage during that busy time. If they still refused to change their plans, I would inform my supervisor and support the decision that ensures the work is handled properly. I would start the project early and aim to complete most of it well before the deadline. This would allow me to handle any unexpected tasks from my boss while still ensuring the project is finished on time and meets high standards. I would feel concerned but remain confident and professional. I would ask my manager for specific examples to better understand the feedback and address any concerns. Even if I disagree, I would focus on improving my performance and demonstrating through my work that I can meet high standards. I would still attend the gathering even if my colleague chooses not to go. It would give me a chance to meet others in the organization, Build professional connections, and show initiative in being involved with the team.

I reschedule the vacation and take the win for looking better professionally. I would also take this opportunity to position myself above my colleague who clearly is not interested in making sacrifices for the company. This will help me get ahead at work and set myself up for a better standing with my higher-ups. As long as I have work to be done, work gets done. I do not wait for pressure to build or deadlines to approach. I have a task, I complete the task, I move onto the next task. When my work is done, I can sit back a bit. I want to always be prepared to take on unexpected tasks instead of trying to jumble too many things because I got too comfortable. I accept the feedback and step up my work. Arguing won't help anything. If I want to continue successfully at the company, I have to play ball and do what I'm told by my superiors. My personal feelings do not dictate my level of professionalism. I encourage my colleague to attend for networking purposes, stating that this will help us professionally and being tired isn't a good reason to pass on a good opportunity. If they still don't want to attend, I attend on my own and try to network with the other people there.

I would negotiate with the other person and the boss so that this doesn't happen again and next time I get the choice of what week to take off and we alternate after that in a fair manner. I would complete the project as soon as possible leaving plenty of time for last minute work if it should arise from the boss. the annual bonus is a big deal so I would approach the boss politely and ask to discuss the issue so that it doesn't happen again and know exactly what the issues are. I would go to the event because connections matter in this business. I might well meet a new client or a new person in the organization who could help me later.

I would refuse too I would tell them to check and see which one put in for that time first The one who did first I would abide by that I would finish on time I would work a set time and quit I woud not stress over it. I am confident whatever comes I will finish on time I would confront the manager and ask how he came up with that conclusion. I would take it to a higher up if we could not resolve it I would let him do whatever he wants. I would go anyway and make new aquantiances. It may further my career to get to know certain people

I'd cite that my manager has just given me another last minute task to complete

I would adjust around my coworkers schedule. I don't think I have concrete plans. It's worth keeping the peace. If I can break out of my introverted bubble, I might meet some interesting people. If not, I will leave early.

I would first have a calm conversation with my colleague and explain that our supervisor already made it clear that both of us can’t be off during that week. I’d try to find a fair solution between us, like seeing if one of us could adjust plans without too much disruption.

If they were still unwilling to change, I would be prepared to move my vacation to another week. While it’s not ideal, making sure the team is properly staffed and the work gets done is more important. In a role like this, reliability and putting the job first matters.

 
I would start making progress on the project right away instead of waiting until the deadline is closer. Even though my workload is light now and I’m confident I could finish it later, I know unexpected tasks can come up from my boss. Getting ahead on the work would give me some flexibility if that happens.

By starting early and pacing the work over the two weeks, I could make sure the project is done carefully and on time while still being available to handle any last-minute assignments.

 I’d probably feel a bit frustrated at first since I don’t agree with the feedback. Still, I’d stay professional and ask my manager for specific examples so I can understand their concerns better. Even if I see it differently, I’d focus on improving and showing through my work that I’m committed to doing the job well.

 Even if my colleague decides to skip the event, I would go on my own. It’s a chance to meet new people, make connections, and show that I’m committed and engaged with the organization. I’d see it as an opportunity to network and learn from others, even if it feels a little out of my comfort zone.

I suggest that the person who has been more productive this quarter gets to take the vacation. The more productive person deserves the break. I come back to my manager with documented proof of my work that shows the opposite of his feedback. I know my manager is wrong, and that my work is always of the best quality. I go, because there is likely important people to know in the organization. It's important to be seen and known by leadership.

I would take the week, and work it. But make sure that this does not happen again, and let myself have more leeway in the future. Avoid conflict
 Immediately inform the boss of the situation, let them know that I will have my momentum derailed by being given tasks at the very end of the work Take it up with them immediately, do not sit idly by and let this happen.  Speak to anyone who can help bring this up Consider how awkward it would be for me to not know anyone, and compile a list of people to get close with at the event that would make networking easy, get background info on people

I would give up that time for vacation to avoid wasting energy on a conflict. The reason being it is too busy to do otherwise. The reason would be to avoid any awkwardness there.

I would opt to change my vacation week.  It's probably not critical that I need to take vacation that exact same week. I would see if it's possible for me to change vacation date and if it's not possible I would also not be willing to budge and risk facing the consequences. I would still go because I'm very sociable and I can make new friends everywhere I go. I would still convince my colleague to go though.

I'd let them take the vacation while I keep working. I'd take advantage of the opportunities they are missing out on that week. I would get the project done right away. I would adhere to their criticisms and make their changes, but I would work to come out the other side looking better. I would skip the outing. I think it would be more important that I get home and recharge for the next day.

i would  discuss it my collegue and try to find a fair solution, i would adjust my schedule to help  the team and keep the work running smoothly
 i would plan my time carefully and focus on completing the project early, this way, i can still handle any urgent tasks from my suvervisor without missing the deadline i would stay calm and listen to the feedback. even if i disagree, i would discuss it with my manager and try to understand their perspective so i can improve my perfomance i would still attend the event, even i if my collegue does not go. it is a good opportunity to meet new people and build professional connections

It would depend on my plans, if I had already made concrete plans somewhere and spent significant money, this is on the supervisor to deal with the work load. It depends on whose plans have priority based on child custody situations and other factors and this study is becoming much longer than what I was originally told. I would be willing to change my plans to make things work for everyone. It is worth it to me to be loyal to the company. I would get the project done as soon as possible. You never know when your workload could increase, so it is worth getting things done early. I would take the feedback and apply it to my work. I think all feedback is valuable in some way, even if I don't agree with it. I would still go to the party. Same. I'd ask my supervisor who asked for the time off first. If my supervisor wanted to solve the issue another way thats fine, but its not really up to me to solve the problem on my own. same. I'd do the best i can, given the constraints. i would put in extra hours if i could or i would ask for help. same. i'd ask them to explain it to me. but as a stockbroker, my annual bonus is probaby really big so i'd want to correct the record if possible same. i'd ask them to go since they owe me one and it would be good networking to be there. i think it would be a good opportunity to climb and connect which you have to do as a stockbroker

I would ask them why they made plans without booking a vacation first and stand firm. Personally I don't think it's productive to encourage coworkers to be lax with their own time management. I would work now to complete the majority of the work so that I can review it. In the meantime if my workload doesn't immediately go up, I can either assist with floating tasks or work on other projects that are going in the background. I would be receptive to their explanations but firm since I am well aware that I'm going above and beyond what is asked of me. I would still go - showing your face is important and developing some level of rapport with colleagues and higher ups is important. I would let my colleague take their vacation and reschedule mine, after all if you love what you do, you will never work a day in your life. I would probably go anyways. Even if I don't know other people there, this is a great way to meet new people and network.

I would be willing to alter my plans if my vacation was not involved in an unchangeable important event, such as a wedding, funeral, birth, etc...  If it was just a regular vacation without unalterable elements, I would switch weeks I would tackle the project right away so that way it would be done and I would not have to worry about it.  I would then have time free to take care of my own issues, or in case the boss springs something on me I would just smile and nod and agree with the boss, as arguing or fighting back would only make things worse.  I would look at what they said I should improve and work on it regardless of reality I am not normally one to go to social events regardless, so I would not attend, especially if it's a function where I don't know anyone, it's not mandatory, and my friend is not attending

I would try and change my plans depending on if there was really no other option and my co-worker is not able to then I would alter my vacation plan. If i had already had my request approved before my colleague even requested theirs, then i would politely challenge this and explain that once my request was approved i would have made arrangements on that basis, and it would feel unfair if this was now cancelled just because someone else has requested it at a later date.

If however the requests were at the same time, I would look to cancel and change my plans if possible and support the team. I would still attend the gathering and would encourage my colleague to do the same, but equally respect their decision if they were very keen to just get home and relax after a long day.

I would reschedule my vacation for another time as I want to be a team player and be supportive of my job.  I would want them to know I value my work. I'd change my plans so they could also take vacation. If I can't take my vacation maybe that will lead to opportunity who knows. Pulling an all nighter for sure. I would see what is stated in the employee handbook regarding vacation requests and time off. It would depend on if i paid for time off. If i did i might just not show up or call out sick, i do not think that if I am not there the company will falter. i would work it cos it looks better on me like im for the company and i would make it know that i am giving up my holiday so expect special treatment in future i would quickly get the work done but not tell anyone so if i get more work i dont have a problem getting it done, but it looks good to my boss that i have done it and my own work i would explain my position providing evidence and ask for more details on the negative feedback so they know my position i go to show my face and leave at a suitable time, i pretend im drinking but have a soft drink so i dont look out of place and im fresh for the morning. also i go to the party confident and introduce myself and network

I would withdraw my vacation request and do my best to meet the obligations I had in front of me. I would put it to my supervisor to decide as its not my job to approve/reject my own time off. I would work the week and take a vacation at another time. I would change my plans for vacation because I'm a team player that's why I've been on other jobs when this has been an issue and I've always acquiesced to the manager suggestion I would handle this project with the utmost work ethic because my boss has given me the responsibility because you trust me and knows that I will do a good job regardless of the time restraint I feel that I probably didn't do a good job and I'm make an offer to my boss that I would redo the work if that would make it better I would initially feel bad but I am confident in my ability to do it better next time What I would do is get dressed and go and be prepared to meet people that I've never met before make friends and acquaintances and network with people. I would see where my collegue is going. I would also see if I could get someone to work my shift . I would let my colleague have their preferred vacation slot. I should be focusing on work, and my presence will lend me an advantage. I would withdraw my vacation request and commit to working that week. It is important that ongoing investigations are properly staffed; improperly staffed investigations lead to poor quality investigations, which risk not finding sufficient evidence to conduct a proper trial. I would reapply for vacation on a different week to demonstrate my commitment to the force. I would arrange a meeting with my boss in order to determine if I may be given these tasks earlier. This way, I can get a head start, so that my workload isn't too heavily weighted right before the due date. Alternatively, I would ask if my boss was happy for me to receive some additional support from a colleague in the following week to ensure that the project deadline is met. I would feel disappointed in this situation. I would ask to arrange a meeting with my manager to discuss the feedback. In this meeting I would hope to present a case for why I believe the feedback is untrue, or provide concrete evidence to prove that is untrue. Failing this, I would appeal to a higher authority within the organisation, to discuss the inaccuracy of the feedback I received. I am happy to receive negative feedback, as it means there is something actionable I can do to improve. However, feedback that contains inaccuracies is neither just, nor does it identify areas upon which I can actually improve. I would attempt to persuade my colleague to attend the social gathering, as a chance to build relationships within the organisation. However, I would not force them to attend. If they were unwilling to go, I would simply attend alone.
