I would focus on the project and ensure that I do complete it in the two weeks.  As my workload is light, I can spend quality time on the project and work on it at a steady pace. I would want to know why I received the negative feedback and acknowledge my wrong doing if that was the situation but if I did nothing wrong.  If the negative feedback was incorrect, I would query why this is so and attempt to remedy the situation by talking things through with my manager. Being invited to a large social gathering is a bonus for me so I would gladly go and spend time with others, even if I don't quite know them at present.  My colleague has made his/her choice not to go but I have autonomy and I have decided to go, to embrace hopefully discovering new opportunities and meeting new people.

I would volunteer to take my vacation on a different day.  Usually 1 week is really not that different from another.  Plus, flexibility and dependability are important parts of the detective job, so this would look good for me. I would get the project done as soon as possible.  This would demonstrate that I can set and complete work within deadlines.  Finishing it early would give me time to make edits and to complete any additional last minute tasks. I would honestly be really annoyed (especially if I reflected on it and still felt that my manager's feedback was not accurate).  I would take the feedback respectfully; however, I would most likely look for another job somewhere else.  It is really important to work with good people that you can respect, since you spend so much time at work.    If there was not other option, I would keep the detective job here (but I would be looking for something else-even if it was a step back with less money). If I was not applying for a job promotion, I would skip it.  I really hate work parties and gatherings outside of the work day.  However, if I was trying to get the promotion, I would go to the gathering and network.  But, I would not stay too long.

Stand my ground to my colleague and my supervisor. I requested vacation time first it seems like, so I would speak to my supervisor about how I am not changing my vacation time, and to figure it out. Do more work earlier than scheduled to make sure that the project is completed and ready well before the due date. I would get a second opinion on the situation from another higher power, and possibly speak to HR about it. I would also gather proof to show that the feedback isn't correct. I would go to do some networking and meet new people, since I love socializing and making new connections with others.

I would see who got approved first. Whoever got approved first should go and the other one should stay back. I would do the project. If I need help I will ask for it, but I will do the project. I think I can do it. I would ask my coworkers if they agree with the feedback. If they dont I will ask for another meeting with my manager to discuss. If that does not go well I will talk to HR. I would not go. for me to be 100 percent at my job. i need alone time away from my job. That way I can feel good about it.

Since it is a situation that requires one of us, I would be willing to sacrifice the week to do what needs to be done. I would ensure I complete the project at an optimal pace to be ready for any new tasks that may come. This way I won't have to worry about rushing anything. I would take some time to speak with the manager whenever possible to discuss the situation as well as gather any information or material to help prove my case. I would still go because making new connections in this situation could prove to be healthy, and I can grow more comfortable working with others in the organization.

I would talk to my coworker about the situation to see if we can come up with a solution. I will see if he really needs the vacation for whatever reason in comparison to mine, whoever has a more "urgent" need will keep it. And maybe next time they will offer to work instead of me if it happens again, vice versa to even things out. I would steadily work on the project and not wait last minute knowing my boss likes to throw curveballs from time to time. If I'm aware of a potenitally big project that could happen simultaneously I will try to finish it early. I would feel upset possibly, but I would try to explain to my boss to the best of my ability that it's not true. If he doesn't believe it sill I would just deal with on my own and do the best, I can in my position regardless. If I feel the consequences is unfair, I would possibly try to get higher ups involved. I would still like to go for my own reasons. While it may be awkward, it could give me great opportunities to learn about the Org and possibly get a better job. The networking online could also lead to different opportunities that could be beneficial to my life overall.

I would take my vacation week no matter what. This is a high-pressure job and i probably need this vacation same as my coworker does. I would work on the project given to me by my boss at the workplace and take additional projects home. I would have to work some overtime but i am confident i would be able to complete both projects. I would stay quiet but start looking for another job. Invented, bad feedback means i am being unjustly set up to be fired anyways. it would be better for me to start looking for a new job before i stay unemployed. I would go on my own, hoping for some networking and job connections. With this kind of job, you never know when you will lose it, so it is good to know different people.

Attempt to further negotiate with the colleague to try and work out why they are so unwilling to change their plans. I would get as much done as soon as possible in order to have time closer to the due date in case something comes up that needs to be addressed. I would feel slighted, and would point to examples of my work that contradict the feedback, then I would do work that would impress their boss. I would go as it is an important networking opportunity, and a good time to get to know others in the organization and how we can synergize better.

I would ask if their plans are already scheduled.  If my plans are non negotiable, I would tell them I can't switch and need them to make the change.  I would tell them I could switch the next time. I would get the project done early to show that I can take charge and that I'm efficient.  If tasks come up later, I would tell them I have the project that takes priority first. I would gather my evidence and schedule a meeting with the manager.  I would ask them to list their reasons for their feedback and show them why it's not accurate. I would go anyway because I don't have a problem socializing on my own.  I would get to know my team.

Depending what I have planned for the vacation, I would probably request the following week off. I don't think that it is worth it to fight over the same week off. I would take this project one day and a time and doing it up to the deadline. I would keep a planner and write down all the tasks I have to do each day. I would feel a little frustrated. However, when receiving this feedback, I would stay quiet and listen to my manager. I would take this feedback and try to improve my work. I would end up not going to the gathering. I am not a big party person and don't mind missing one social gathering.

I would try to have a conversation with the colleague and with my supervisor to negotiate. For example, if we have to take different weeks of vacation, can we agree that if one person compromises this time, they get the first pick next time when there is a conflict? I would start work on it immediately and have it a solid working state so that I do not have to worry or stress when the due date comes. I would seek advice and perspective from colleagues and other managers, if possible. If the feedback is warranted, I would want to change. However, if it is unfairly given, then I would want to secure my bonus and avoid negative consequences unnecessarily. I'd also start looking for another position. I would not go - it's great to socialize with others professionally, but there is wisdom in knowing your limits and after a long day, it's time to rest.

It depends on the plans I've made and who I've made them with. If I made the plans for just my self, I'll be willing to change my plans. Otherwise, no. I always do my work as early as I can so I can have time to go over it and check for errors or where can be improved. I typically avoid confrontation but if it carries heavy consequences I will speak up and talk to my manager about why I disagree with his feedback. If my colleague won't go, neither will I. I'm not a very social person so I avoid situations where I have to socialize with completely unknown people without a familiar face.

I would have a conversation with them and understand their reasoning behind their decision. If I'm flexible with my schedule, I would choose another week off, but if not, I would ask my supervisor to step in and help make the decision. I would ask the boss for some more time and consider taking things to the higher ups if his actions persist after the conversation. I would try to understand where he's coming from and just redo the work to his liking to avoid losing my bonus. I would try to persuade them into going, but if they persist on nnot going, I would give it a try on going alone. Depending on how well things go, I would stay or leave early.

I would change my plans and play the peacemaker.  I think it will give me points with the supervisor. I would complete the project as soon as possible, just to get it out of the way.  It will give me peace of mind. I would speak calmly with my supervisor, and I would also  present evidence of my good work.  This will help my case.
 I would go , I would like to get to know the colleagues better. I also think it will help me in the future.

I would not change my vacation and stay at work to keep the peace and prevent further disagreements. I would work ahead to get the project done because i know that my boss will give me an extra project. I would confront the manager by setting up a meeting to go over their feedback and would defend myself. I would probably try to go because in this industry I think connections are very important and i could meet people

I would change my vacation plans. My job is more important than vacation, which I can take anytime. I wouldn't want the team to get behind with two people gone. I would keep working on the project and finish it up as quickly as possible. I would let my boss know that I am light on work and could use more. I would work harder to correct the perceived negative situation. I would ask my boss for more clarity on what exactly I need to do to do better the next time. I would go to the gathering and network with as many people as I possibly could. I would try to seek out power players and meet them since I don't already know them.

I would go ahead and change my vacation plans because then only would that look good in the eyes of the company, the other employee is going to owe me for doing it. By nature, I'd like to get things done as soon as possible. I will take on this project, get it completely done, and then by chance if there's extras that my boss adds on, and I have plenty of time to do it. I would be very upset about the situation, especially the feedback is that true. I would talk to my manager, and then if we can't come to some kind of compromise that's very good day for me, and reflects what actually happened, then I'm gonna go to both of our media supervisors. I would definitely go to this gathering. This would be a excellent chance to get to know others in the organization, and build on my network.

I would speak with the colleague and let them know that you have no issues with staying on the schedule so they can follow through with their plans of taking a vacation. Vacation time will always find a way but your job is important. I would get started on the project right away and work until it is completed. A project that you have two weeks to finish should not wait until the last minute. If you wait then there is possibility something will get in the way or you will have to rush. I would immediately question how the feedback decision was decided upon. If I feel like it is true I would immediately change how I handled things. Even if I did not feel like it was true of my character I would make sure I understood what was considered wrong and fix it according to how my manager needed it to be. I would definitely plan on going. You cannot get anywhere in life nor in your career by depending on someone to accompany you every where you have to be when it is part of your job.

I would say myself and my colleague would and could come to an arrangement about who will change their plans. I realize this is most likely a family issue for both sides also and not just up the us. I would say if this didnt work we would let our supervisor decide or flip a coin or something. I would strive to have it ready before the two weeks if possible. I would also work on it on my time if possible. Most likely I would go along to get along. He is the manager and has more say than myself. I wouldn't like loosing my bonus ,that would piss me off. I would try to get my colleague to come along. If that didn't work out I would go anyways. I could get to know new people when there.

I would change my vacation plans. It would not be much of a big deal for me unless I had something already planned out that is extremely important during that week. I can always schedule my vacation for another week. I would start this project ahead of time. In doing this, I would be able to take my time with the project, stay organized, and be accurate. The project would be done with effort, high quality, and carefully. I would feel a bit disappointed, but I would take the steps I need to take to satisfy my manager. Since I do not agree with the feedback given to me, I would ask specific questions to get a better perspective. I have to be open minded. I would go to the event whether or not my colleague decides to go. It's the perfect way to bond with my colleagues and it keeps me involved with company gatherings.

As long as the other colleague requested the week off first, I'd be perfectly ok with it. I understand the needs of the company and am always willing to be flexible. I would request time off on another week I would work ahead and strategize. I would make that the project is due ahead of the deadline. Then I would be prepared for whatever additional tasks fall into my purview. I would acknowledge my manager's sentiments and then calmly ask questions about the situation. I would remain extremely calm and conciliatory. I would provide concrete examples that contradict his or her notion of the situation. I think it would be an excellent opportunity to get to know new colleagues. I'd be willing to risk a little awkwardness to get to know people. I would likely go to the gathering.

If I was able to, I would change my plans to accommodate my coworker.  I am generally flexible and willing to accommodate others, and I usually only plan staycations.  In the future, I would coordinate in advance so that this wouldn't happen again. I would get started right away.  Since my work load is currently light and I'm not certain it will remain this way, I should get the project done while I have the time. I am dissappointed to receive negative feedback that I don't believe is true. I would ask for more information from the manager and see if they have evidence.  I would share my point of view. I usually try to attend these events even if I only stay for a short time because the networking is important.

I would not be willing to budge and instead insist on getting my way. What makes this other person so special that they get to take vacation over me. I have done all that I've been asked and as such, am entitled to a vacation I was already scheduled for. I would take things slow because I know the project will succeed. Every project I've ever been a part of has been a success. Two weeks is ample time to complete this project. There will be "last minute tasks"? How hard can they be? I'll deal with those issues as they arise - they don't scare me. I would definitely call them out on that. No one is going to get away with disrespecting me, especially with a bonus on the line. I can not and will not tolerate people trying to ruin my reputation or sabotage my career. I would show up at the manager's office and give them a tongue chewing. Why would I bother going there? I don't know anyone there so they are not useful to me. I have more important things I could be doing than participating in these childish outings. I will not waste my time with this.

In this case, I would emphasize the caseload that I currently have and continue to ask for that week I requested off. I would offer it up to the supervisor to choose who is best needed that week, and to then let me know whether or not I can take that week off or not. I planned this week according to my expected caseload and court dates for my cases. I would plug ahead with the project as if it were do now, and then set it aside if some last minute crucial tasks arise. If I start now, I will not have as much to do later. I can get part of it done now, and then I can plan ahead for the next steps. When you have a light workload, you work in advance. In this case, it is best to schedule a follow-up meeting with the supervisor rather than wait and let things brew and affect me. I would figure out what it is they did not like, and then show examples of similar tasks where I did exemplary work. Perhaps that will make the feedback less permanent in my record. I am going to not go to the social gathering, so that I can rest up for the next day. I am more interested in performing well than socializing. I can meet the new people in the breakroom or out on task in working groups or something down the road.

I would negotiate with my colleague to get them to change their vacation plans. I would offer them something in return for me to get this vacation week. This is an opportunity for the entire workplace to be satisfied. My colleague can get something they need or want from me that they do not have. I can get my desired vacation week. My supervisor and workplace do not have to be too busy during that week. I would handle this project by completing it first. It is important to get things done in an orderly and timely fashion. Getting this project out of the way first frees up time and energy to complete the tasks my boss gives me. If I am still in the process of completing this project while my boss gives me a new task, I would also enlist a colleague to help me. I feel that this is unfair and unacceptable that something so obviously untrue should negatively affect my career. I would go to my manager and ask for explicit documentation and reasoning for the feedback. I would present documentation supporting my reasoning. If my manager still disagrees, I would ask for help from colleagues or other higher-ups to support me in defending myself. I would try to convince my colleague to attend. If the colleague still does not want to attend, I would go by myself. I want to be known as someone who is dependable and someone who is known by the people in the organization. I want to make my presence known to leverage myself for future career opportunities. This is a networking event, and I am willing to show that I am committed to making a name for myself.

I would argue my point about being off this week and why It's more important for me to have it off then my colleague. I feel like Im more deserving of this week off and have earned it. I would try and find out what might possibly come up and run it by my boss and get ahead of the issue before it becomes last minute. I want to be ahead of the project, not get last minute deadlines on some issues. Im not happy with this situation and think the negative feedback is undeserved. I would request another meeting with my manager to discuss the issue and have a list of reasons to support my stance. If they fail to listen, I will go above them. i would talk them into going, doing whatever is necessary to convince them to go, even if it for a brief period of time. I could get them to introduce me to some of the other people. I want to meet other people and expand my acquaintances.

I would just change my vacation week if able. There's no need to start an argument with a coworker over vacation time. I could just take a different week off. I would complete the project in a timely manner, giving me time to go over it if needed before it's due. It would also free up time to complete any new tasks that land on my desk. I would be upset but stay level-headed. I would ask my manager what I could do to fix what they find negative. I would listen to what they had to say and think of ways I could adapt while also staying true to myself. I would ask them to go just for a little bit with me, that way we have each other to hang out with. It would be good for both of us to mingle with our coworkers that we're not familiar with. It could help us later on to develop those friendly relationships. It doesn't hurt just to go for an hour or two.

I would ask my supervisor if it would be fair to give the week off to the first person that asked for it. I would make sure to set time aside each day to work on the project. This would mean making it a priority, especially when other duties have been done. I would ask a coworker and/or other manager what they thought of my job performance. If it is not negative from them, I wouldn't take my manager seriously. I would not go. I know that being at a social gathering while I'm tired would not bring out the best of me and it's best I'm not around others.

I would insist that I take the vacation time and that the other person work that week because I always attend to my prior commitments and I want the company to thrive. It only makes sense for them to work. I would simply get the project done because I know it has to be completed anyway. I wouldn't waste time worrying about getting another assignment or not. I would attend to the negative feedback by trying my best to find a way for myself to interpret it in an effective way. I would feel it is simply my duty to try and better my work with advice from others. I would go whether or not they want to join me. I would do this because I am a solo worker who needs to take charge of my own career trajectory. If I don't know people there, it will change once I introduce myself and make conversation.

I would find out who has been employed there longer, the colleague or me. Seniority level can be a fair way to determine who gets that vacation week. I would talk to my boss about how I am feeling. I would share my feelings about getting last (minute)? tasks. P.S. I think you are missing a word in your question above. I would write down an account of the negative feedback. I would then write a summation of positive things that have happened within the same time frame that completely contradict the negative feedback. I may even go line by line of the negative feedback and provide a contradictory comment or concern. If I really didn't want to go, I wouldn't go. I don't like being around a lot of different people in a short amount of time, anyway. Or, I might just go for a short bit to make an appearance.

I would challenge when the vacation request was submitted and decide from there.  Also, I would tell my supervisor to grow a backbone and make a decision. Get it done, meet with the stakeholders, ensure it meets their satisfaction, and move towards the next milestone.  I would question my boss's ability to manage themselves if they repeatedly assign late tasks without justification. Considering I just got the project done quickly and within the guidelines, the manager better have some facts to back up the negative feedback.  Facts, then feelings, bro (or sis). I would ask for the details I may have missed and work to fix as necessary. I would go for a little while (about 30 minutes) and then leave.  Why?  Because you never know what networking you can get done with people you don't know.  But because it is also stock brokers, I don't want to hear a lot about work either.

It depends on what my vacations for and how much flexibility I have around booking/travel times, but I would probably switch weeks I would start planning early so I'm prepared for extra work that's to be expected especially if this has happened before I would explain to him why I feel the way I feel and ask him to elaborate on what I did wrong and how I can Improve for future performance I would try to convince my friend but if they persist I would go alone and try and connect/socialise with others there

Say that the leave should be granted to whoever applied for it first.  That's showing my leadership qualities in a fair manner. Create a project plan and list of tasks and dependencies, and get it approved by my  boss. Call out the risk that any new last minute tasks will result in the deadline being missed. I'd arrange a meeting with my manager to discuss the feedback in detail.  Did someone raise a concern?  Is the feedback based on quantifiable data?  I would want to prove that i did nothing wrong. Insist that they go and point out the benefits to both of you of going.  If necessary allude to future positive impact of going.

I would stay on and change my own date, as this could be an opportunity to demonstrate my dedication over my colleague who is prioritising their holiday.
This could lead to future opportunities by getting ahead of my colleague while they are away. Make sure that the current project is completed ahead of schedule. I would want to ensure that I have actioned this as quickly as possible to give myself more time in case of future projects. I would feel upset by this if the feedback is incorrect. I would go above my manager to the next highest level of management to explain my position and why my manager is incorrect in their assessment. I would also probably not go. Being social is not necessary to my job and my effectiveness and I wouldn't want to waste my own time by attending something I have no interest in.

Make decision based on who has put in their application first, if all else is equal. Otherwise, delay own holiday so that work is not negatively affected. Get ahead with the work in advance, find out if there are potentially future tasks coming up that could be done sooner. Talk to manager about the feedback and make them aware that I don't agree, with clear points on why that is the case. Go home and rest, so that I am ready to continue work the next day and not negatively affected by being too tired.

Make it the manager's problem. Staffing issues are not my concern. As long as we have both given sufficient notice of our vacations plans, we should both be able to go. Let management figure it out. I would assume that I will get these last-minute tasks and clear my schedule accordingly. I would follow up with my manager to give me my tasks piecemeal as they get them, so I can mitigate the amount of last-minute dumping I have to deal with. I would ask for specific examples of how they think I mishandled things, so I can see if there's truly something there. If they can't back up the negative feedback with receipts, they should not be giving it. Go anyway and try to make connections with others that may benefit me goingforward. Try to dip out relatively early but make sure I am "seen".

I would change my plans so as to appear more flexible to my boss, and to show that I can be reliable when called upon. I would try to approach my boss to see if there are any larger future tasks that I could tackle now, to avoid having a heavier workload at the end of the project. I would still try to parse out what part of the feedback could be actionable, and try to change my approach to doing things based off of what the feedback was. I would still go, as it would help me appear as a more sociable coworker, and I would have more networking opportunities at the event.

I would take a vacation the week after my colleague did. The reason why is to avoid conflict with the colleague who wants a vacation a particular week. I feel this is the best compromise in a situation such as this one. I would begin the project two weeks before it is due despite having a light workload. The reason why is because the project may be completed by the due date if I started it earlier instead of a few days before it's due. I would feel unhappy about the situation. But, I would handle it in the most civilized way possible. I would ask my manager why they gave me negative feedback. Then, I would take notes on what I need to improve and try to do better before the next evaluation. If I handle the situation this way, I would possibly maintain my bonus and satisfy my manager at the same time. If my colleague isn't going to this social gathering, it would be best if I don't go as well. The reason why is because I wouldn't know anyone there and don't think anyone would introduce themselves to me if I went.

I would explain why it is more advantageous for the company for me to be gone on the week, versus my co-worker being gone. I would only worry about myself and the business, not what my co-worker thinks. I would do the project as soon as I am able to and ask for additional projects to prove my efficiency and capability. I don't agree with my manager and would explain why I feel the way I do. I would provide specific examples to refute the feedback. I would go to the large social gathering because it's important to mingle with all members of the organization, especially because you never know when a new job opening is coming up in a different department.

I would discuss the problem with my supervisor. I believe what would be most fair would be to reward whoever has the best performance with the requested vacation week, and I would be confident that my work record could secure me the spot. I would start the project when I was given it, but I would not feel compelled to rush through it either. I excel at multitasking and wouldn't struggle to juggle this project as well as the ones I may be given later in the week. I would respectfully request to discuss the critique further with my manager before I made a point to implement it. If I strongly believed it wasn't true, I would want to first make sure there hadn't been some misunderstanding between the two of us. I would go regardless. I wouldn't want to miss an opportunity to network. I would also encourage my colleague to do the same, valuable as those opportunities are.

I would acquise because that is the type of person I am but I would be mad about it and maybe complain to my supervisoer. But I am the type of person to sacrifice for others. I would do it. It's my job to do work and I would get it done. I priotize being on top of things and getting stuff done. I would talk it out with him / her again and I would ask for proof to show me that. Like for example if they said I was late, I would ask for data, time stamps, whatever to show that I wasn't. I would like evidence. I wouldn't go. I am not in the mood to feel uncomfortable and would rather go home. If she wasn't going, I am sure there will be other gatherings,

I would first ask the colleague what their vacation includes, to where they are going and who else will be there. i will then present my case and why this vacation is essential to me. hopefully we can come to an agreement if not i would propose the idea to let our boss decide who will go first and who will go second. I would ultimately try to reach a solution between the two of us first. I would first approach my boss and ask him if he is aware that my schedule is tight. That i would be able to complete my workload without having last minuet additional projects. i would ask him to help prioritize my to do list so that things will be handled in a manner that is satisfactory to him. I would specifically ask my manager to provide his reasons for the feedback and ask him what i could do to improve my work. I would do some research into what or how i could perform better on my own and mix the two. I would try harder to make sure my work is superior. I would indeed go. I would network and meet new people. I would propose some of my ideas and contributions to the company and collaborate with others. I would let my friend know i was going anyway and that i would fill him in.

Ask my supervisor who placed in the request first and whoever made the request first should be the one that goes on vacation. I would do that because that seems far. first come first serve. Go ahead and do what i need to do to complete the project and turn it in now just in case. Because i would not want to stress myself out if i have multiple projects due at the same time. I would be upset. I would talk with my manager to see what i would need to do to fix the situation. I would not go. It does not seem that it would be mandatory. Plus I had a long day at work so I'm going home.

I would offer to change my vacation week to the week after so that the business is not disrupted by our absences. I would proactively speak to my manager and ask them if they had any other tasks in mind befoer the due date. I would tell them that my work load is currently light and I can take on more work if needed. I would ask my manager to schedule a one-on-one where we can discuss the given feedback. I would bring up my concerns how it is unreflective of my actual performance and give examples. I would make a point to still go to the social gathering alone. This would allow me to build relationships with others in my organization that will help me develop in my role and career in the future.

If I already have bought tickets or invested money in these plans I would not change. On the other hand, if nothing was invested quite yet I would go ahead and change my plans. Because as long as nothing is set in stone yet changing when I take my vacation is not that big of a deal to me as long as I get to take my vacation. I would put in a solid effort on the project and not relax just because I have a light workload. I would use this time to make steady progress while not overworking myself, planning on getting this project done on time or even early. Allowing for the possibility of last minute tasks that will be able to fit in my schedule with only slightly more effort on the main project. I would feel frustrated at the situation, and would ask to have a meeting with my supervisor. Allowing me to have a calm and respectful conversation to be able to explain my perspective and hear out my managers perspective as well. I would go since even after a long day at work it is nice to be able to unwind and have fun. Even though I don't know anyone this would potentially allow me to network and meet new people as well.

I would see if I can move my stuff and just ultimately go from t here, If I could not, I would sit down with the coworker and hash out what we need to do. I would stay on top of my project just in case something else where to come up. You know what they say, if you stay ready you do not have to get ready when challenges arise. I would talk to my manager, and I would ask why I was given this feedback. I would want to know what I did to get such a negative feedback cause it wouldn't make me happy. I honestly probably would not go myself, if they are not going to go, I don't think that I would want to go.

I would not bother to compete or make issue over the weeks leave and withdraw my vacation leave request to prevent any tension within the workplace. I would ensure the earliest completion of my assigned project to allow free time if in the event additional assigned tasks are given to me. This ensures I meet set deadlines and am still available to assist with unexpected workload. I would accept the managers feedback and not contest it. I would do self guided research on how to improve in the areas for which I received negative feedback. I would do this to show personal growth without adding any additional tasks  to my manager via request for guidance. I would attend the event regardless of my friends ability to attend with me so that I can foster and develop new friendships with colleagues. This could potentially lead to a robust set of contacts for future career growth.

If I would not lose a significant amount of money by changing/cancelling my plans, I would be willing to change my vacation plans to different dates.  I would express that I would hope that in the future I would be given precedence for my vacation picks for my willingness to change my previously approved vacation time off.  They could "owe me one". I always do whatever work I have in front of me.  As long as all other projects were taken care of and I had nothing more pressing to be done first, I would work on and complete this project as soon as possible.  Something can always come up and knowing that my boss tends to bring last minutes tasks to the table, it just makes sense to get it done while I have the time. I would ask for clarification on what the issue was.  Many times the problems are arising from a lack of or a miscommunication between people.  If expectations are not clearly stated problems can crop up and people can feel let down.  If this still did not clear the situation up, I would want a third party review.  Either someone else in management or HR should be brought in who has familiarity of the situation so that they could assist. I would go.  Even if it is was just for a half an hour.  I think it is important, especially as someone who is new to the company, to get to know everyone else in the company.  Putting yourself out there and meeting people is part of business and networking.  I will not always be able to rely on my co-worker to be introduce me to people.

I would ask for the reason they cant move their vacation and then secondly find a reason why my vacation is more important or why I should be off that week and not them. Ex: seniority, cost of my vacation, asking manager before the other person, etc. I would work to finish the project early. If I end up getting a last minute change or request, I would be able to handle it and still finish the project on time. If I dont get a request like that, I would seek out additional work anyway from my manager to fill the time and show my skills I would change the conversation to focus on my strengths to get my manager back on board with my performance. I would highlight any projects I did, any KPIs I achieved, and how it impacts the company. If that didnt work, I would start talking about the performance of others in relation to my own to make me appear superior I would go regardless of my coworkers decision and use it as a networking opportunity to meet new people.

I would reach out to the colleague and come to a compromise, perhaps trade favors to allow them to take their preferred week in exchange for helping me on a project or to learn a tool. This way we both can feel like we obtained something and the supervisor has the proper coverage for that week. I'd wrap the project details up to the best of my abilities now so I could handle last minute requests, but also be proactive in reaching out to my boss to see if they anticipate additional tasks. That way I can allocate my time effectively and get the project done with steady work and no last minute rushing. I would ask questions about the feedback. What observations was it based on? Then I would explain context if needed and ask for a re-evaluation based on all pertinent facts. If the manager is unwilling to budge, I'd be firm in asking for how the scenario should have been handled to get a better review. I would go on my own, but prepare for the event by looking up any special guests or keynote speakers ahead of time. This would allow me to come up with conversation starters, and also perhaps narrow in on people that I want to network with or at least introduce myself to at the event. In short, I wouldn't be dissuaded from going, but I would go in with a plan and not just wing it.

I would tell my colleague that they should strongly reconsider moving their plans to another time, and if not, escalate the situation to my supervisor and explain why I should be allowed to take the vacation, and not my colleague. If the supervisor does not agree, I would ask that I get to take some of my colleague's cases over in return for giving up my vacation week, getting me further ahead of them. I would prioritize finishing the project in the event that my boss decides to throw any extra tasks my way. I would provide proof that the feedback is inaccurate and that my feelings towards it are justified, and calmly let the manager know that they made a mistake. This would politely show them that it wouldn't be a good idea to repeat that mistake, as I always bring proof. I would go to the social gathering regardless of if my colleague is attending or not, since there is the potential to mingle with other people higher up in my organization, which could lead to getting ahead, and eventually a promotion of some sort.

I would switch weeks and show how amazing I am at doing the colleagues' work while they are out. I desire getting ahead and I am not afraid to move vacation time to do so. I would get the work done ahead of schedule to show how efficient I am and how much my boss can rely on me. That way, late tasks can still be done in a timely manner. I would show that the negative feedback was untrue by proving myself. Whatever they said about me, I can prove the opposite is true. I would go. I would network and meet new people, and my colleague would miss out. I would be the life of the party and make new friends.

I would stand firm, citing non-refundable tickets or a flight, or an unmovable event like a wedding or funeral. If my colleague refuses to budge, our supervisor will have to find coverage (technically this is what they are legally supposed to do in this situation, anyhow). I would continue to work on the project at a decent pace, ramping up toward the deadline. If my boss comes to me with additional last-minute tasks, I will decline, citing my impending deadline. I would ask for more information to understand their position, then gently push back without being defensive. If I feel the situation is too heavy in the moment, I'd step back, think hard and write some notes, then return so we can continue the discussion after we've both had time to digest the information. I am sure we could come to a resolution of whatever the conflict is. Depending on the circumstances of the event (time, location, how much effort required to get there) I'd likely still go for the sake of networking and just to be able to say I made an appearance. I can always leave early if it's not a fit!

I'd go to my coworker and tell them that I wont volunteer to change either. We can either let a game of luck decide who gets to go, or let our boss decide, but we should figure out a way to determine it between us. I'd try to complete the work early in order to contribute to any other projects that might come up. I'd offer my services to other employees if any other projects need help. I would bring as much data as I could, and make a case for why my performance has been better. But I might also start looking at other jobs, and figure out where I can be better appreciated. I would go to the event whether my friends went or not, and network to meet new people. Growing a network of colleagues and coworkers can be really valuable.

I would take my vacation on a different week as this would be good for the company, and I could spend more time working on cases that are assigned to me.  I would just take it at a more convenient time. I would get started on the project right away and finish as soon as I could so I could get a start on other work if need be. I would try to clear the air right away and have a meeting with my manger. This way the situation could get corrected as soon as possible and we could continue on in a productive working environment. I would go to the social gathering and get better acquainted with my collogues. This would be a great way for me to get to know some of my new colleagues.

I would speak to the colleague and let them know that my vacation is non-refundable and that I am unable to accommodate them. I will need to take my vacation as it has been scheduled for some time. I would work on the project in a timely manner. I would take on the tasks that I am able to take own without causing a negative result with the project. I would explain to the boss my situation. I would speak to my manager and ask them to explain in detail what the issue was and I would explain my side. I would want them to know that I do not feel that I agree with the feedback. I would chose to skip the after hours social gathering as I have a busy afternoon of important things I need to take care of at home.

I would not change my plans. My time is important and I deserve the break. I worked hard for the vacation. I would work hard to finish the tasks in the given amount of time. I would spend all day and night to finish. I would confront my manager directly to see why they gave me the feedback and ask for specific examples. I would still go to network and meet new people. I can make new connections that can help me out for my job and my future.

I may cancel vacation plans if I cant get the week I requested. If I was in need of advancing in this job, I feel it is more important to stick with work. I would try to use time management skills as best as I can. Possibly working overtime if my boss would consider a raise. I would discuss with them why I don't agree, and try to come up with a compromise. If the issue remains unresolved, I would ask help with people that are higher up. I might attempt to go to the social gathering for networking. I think if it is important to advance in this job, this could help me out.

I would still take my vacation. I would take it because it's not my problem that the company is short staffed because me and another colleague are off at the same time. I would handle the project per usual. If my boss gives a last-minute task, I would ask for either more time or I would request that I need to finish my original project first before taking on another. I would plead my case and provide proof that I am not guilty. In addition, I would want an apology for the stain on my reputation. It would make me look incompetent. I would only go for the sake of advancing my career and for my comfort. I would persuade my colleague that they may want to do the same, and why it would be beneficial in the long run.

I would change my plans to accommodate the work environment, because I believe it is mutually beneficial. I think a responsible work environment would take care of employees who do so, knowing that they are keeping the business running during this time and the employee is willing to do what it takes to make sure everything is covered. I would make sure I plan ahead to have time for both this project, and anything else that may come up. The worst case scenario is that I would have an emergency project and already have time reserved for it, but if nothing happens, I would have time to finish this initiative early. I would try to get more information about this feedback to see what I could do differently in the past. I would treat this as a way to get more information and improve my performance overall. I think everything has the opportunity to be a learning event. I am always willing to try new things and meet new people. I would go anyway and treat this as a networking event to foster new relationships.

I would try and be flexible and change vacation days if possible, as I believe this is what would be most appealing to an employer. I would have regular meetings with the boss or project lead in order to track progress and find out the last tasks as soon as possible. I would discuss this with colleagues and my boss in order to better understand the decision and to obtain positive feedback. I would likely go in order to socialise with the team and get to know them, and encourage my colleague to dot he same.

I would request that the first person to request time off be allotted the time off.  It is only fair and makes sense.  If had submitted first and they are not willing to change, then I would want to be compensated in some way for me to change. I would finish this project as soon as possible, so I am prepared for any impromptu projects.  I would want to ensure that I have enough time to accomplish all of my tasks. I would feel worried and very concerned.  I would also be hurt that they believe this untrue thing about me.  I would ask for validity in the feedback to verify it is certainly true.  If it is not, then I am good.  If it is true, then I would work to change to fix to problem. I would attend the social gathering, at least to make an appearance.  It could be a good time and it could be a good networking event.  If it is not, then it is no problem and I can leave at any time.

I would move my vacation to another date to preserve my job, and ask for more flexibility from my superiors in the future. If I'm more concerned with getting ahead, I think being cooperative would help. I would finish the job as quickly as possible so I have time and space for additional last minute projects. I would want my boss to see that I can stay ahead of tasks since being a detective is challenging and time consuming. I would take the feedback and move on, regardless of whether I could do anything about it. I would want to continue to push through and would expect this in this working environment. I would go home, I have too much work to do the next day and wouldn't see socializing as an integral part of my job.

I would accept the fact that my colleague will not change and change my date. I would show I am the bigger person and respect those I work with. I would also do this to leave the good impression on my supervisor so I can get promoted or make more money I would make sure to start this project as soon as possible and do all I can do finish fast while still being effective and doing well. I would do this because like it says, you never know when something more significant or important could pop up, so use time wisely and just do my job I would have a discussion with him regarding why he gave me the feedback and what I can do to change it. I would set expectations with my manager as well so they aren't giving negative feedback on things they shouldn't I would go. I believe in team chemistry, and even though I wouldn't know anyone, I could meet new colleagues and network while I am there to make connections and be a step ahead in moving up or advancing

I would continue with my vacation plans by convincing my colleague to reschedule if that fails i will allow the supervisor decide who goes and who postpones their vacation to a different week because i am in the same situation as my colleague and hence not the best person to decide in this case. I will complete this project ahead of time since my workload is light and leave room because of the possibility of any last tasks that i may be assigned that will take significant amount of time. Hence sure to complete all my assigned projects within scheduled time. This situation feels bad, i would respectfully seek audience with my manager, collect and present any evidence i have on the contrary during this meeting to enable my manager see the true picture and reconsider the feedback that i believe is not true. I would attend the social gathering with others in the organization regardless of my colleague's decision, because i would use this opportunity to interact with others that i currently do not know and possibly create new friendships.

If I could take a different week off that would not change any of my plans or increase the costs, or negatively impact what I wanted in any way then I would gladly change my week. But if I had to make any sacrifices I would suggest that we find an impartial way to decide who changes their vacation week. I would work on the project as much as possible every day until it was done. I would use the extra time to work on other projects and anticipate any last minute tasks. I would take the time to compile all the data and reasons as to why this feedback was not fair. I would then present it to my manager in a respectful way. I would see if my colleague would want to have a small bite to eat and a coffee to get some energy so that we could go to the gathering. If I could not convince my colleague then I would just attend on my own and make new friends.

I would speak to my colleague directly an negotiate the terms for them to switch their vacation week. I would use all knowledge I have of them from over time to appeal to their personal and professional values. I would take care of my other obligations to devote more time to work. I would make my life flexible for the purpose of being able to respond to any urgent final tasks. I would escalate one step above my manager and instigate a higher-level look at my manager's actions. I would raise any evidence I have of retaliation and bring out past records of conflicting positive reviews. I believe in my own ability to make a good experience out of a bad one. I would attend alone and find an existing group to inject myself into.

i would change my vacation time to ensure that everything goes smoothly and there are no disruption. i would start as early as possible to make sure that i have sufficient time to get everything done. I would sit with my supervisor and try to understand their feedback. I would bring proof of why I dont believe that is true to help save my professional reputation. i would still go so i am able to meet other people in the organization. I wouldnt want to miss the chance on networking with others

Try to use leverage and/or bartering to get what I want. Because I was given the goal of getting ahead so that seems to be the most reasonable. Alternatively, cancel my vacation and use that to make myself look like the better person, again to leverage that to my advantage. Again, I'm going to finish early and pretend to be busy until the due date; however, this time I may also try to anticipate what my boss might ask of me so as to appear extra capable. I'm either going to be a kiss ass about it or I am going to change their mind in a way that makes them feel like we worked together as a team. That could go either way, depends entirely on which outcome I feel would have the most personal benefit.

I think based on what we read about the stockbroker mentality... I would probably just let her take the vacation time and I would reschedule mine for a different time. If it is not a great time to take a vacation for the firm then I can take one for the team! Hopefully, I didn't have anything important planned for that week. I would make sure that my light workload is all completed. If it isn't something super heavy then why even space it out? I can complete the work right away and if my boss has something for me later in the week, I won't be overwhelmed because everything else will be completed. I would ask for the negative feedback in writing and if I don't agree with it, I would compile my evidence for why I don't agree with it. I would then likely talk to the manager again and see if we can come to a resolution. If we can't, I would go to HR. If it actually has a chance of impacting my bonus then I think I should be treated fairly with a reassessment of my work. I would probably just go. I'm an extroverted person and I could go for a bit and try and meet some new people. If I am not having fun, I can just leave.

My first goal would be to try to find a compromise with the colleague. Could we split the week and/or trade holidays working so we could make something fair for both of us? My goal would be to have the project done as quickly as I can while also producing high-quality work. My goal would be to have the work done three to five days before the deadline, so I have time to handle unexpected last-minute tasks. First, I would make sure I am calm and regulated, so that I can evaluate the feedback logically. Then, I would debrief the feedback with a colleague who I believe knows me and my work well. I would try to find some areas of the feedback I can use. I would then ask for another meeting with my manager to ask more questions, explain my concerns, and offer clarity. I would go regardless. I think it is important to nextwork with others in the company, as collaboration often makes us all more effiecnt and successful. I would go with the goal of meeting at least two new people who might relate to my role.

I would maintain my plans and challenge my coworker to a productivity based competition. This would ensure fairness in the prioritization of who gets the week off and could potential show my work in a good light. I would plan on finishing the project with 7-5 days to spare. This was I could work in a set timetable and be able to handle any boss requests adequately. I would be upset. I would find a way to maintain my benefits, whether that be going above my manager to display my results or proving them wrong with future work. I would go to the event. This would be an opportunity to create connections that could later come in handy with my peers.

I would negotiate with the colleague - this time, they get their way, but next time our vacation requests are conflicting, it will be my turn. If they don't agree to this, I will let them know that I'm going to involve our supervisor as an arbitrator. I would tackle the task while my workload is light, rather than waiting until closer to the due date. I would want the extra time for doing my best work and then being free for any last minute tasks my supervisor assigns. Negative feedback that I disagree with would feel like a slight. I would be somewhat affronted by that. However, in the interest of improving myself and being respectful, I would prepare specific questions for my manager to further understand their feedback. During that discussion, if I still disagree, I would calmly share my differences of opinion. The manager may or may not change their mind by the end of our discussion; however, it is more likely they will acknowledge that this can be seen in more than one way and less likely that this will affect me negatively in things like losing a bonus. I would let them make their own decision about attendance. If they attend, we would have a further opportunity to bond which can make our work together stronger. Without them there, I would also have extra incentive to meet new people. Making new connections within the organization is likely to be positive in my career.

I would change my vacation plans to accommodate the coworkers plans as well as making sure the agency is staffed and able to handle the work load. There are 51 other weeks in the year I can move my schedule around. I would work on it and finish it as soon as I am able. Getting ahead and having spare time to accomplish added tasks will keep me focused on my work and not falling behind because of procrastination. I would not feel great about the situation and I wouldn't accept it as final. I would request to talk to my manager and figure out the exact problem and then, with my manager, work towards a goal that would fix the issues for the future. I would go if my coworker was going. I am not one to go to parties by myself, I prefer my home or at least going with friends/coworkers that I could have a good time with.

I'd tell my boss they would have to figure something out because they can't control when I take vacation I would finish it all up front because he might give me more work and I don't want to be saddled with work at the last moment I would talk to another manager about it or his manager to try to amend the situation and get a more fair evulation of how I am doing I would still go because why should they influence what I do I just wanna get drunk and have fun how is that not 100 characters

I would opt to take a different vacation week. Since it is clear my colleague will not change their plans, it would be necessary for someone to be there. I would choose to be there myself to ensure the work was handled. I would focus on working quickly and steadily on the project, especially at the beginning of the 2 weeks. I would try to finish as much as possible in the first week in case any last minute tasks came up. I would feel upset and frustrated. I would go above my manager to dispute the feedback. I would not just accept it since it could have such negative consequences. I would go there on my own. I could make some other important connections there so that I would know others beyond my one colleague.

I would talk to my colleague and ask them again. If they still say they are not gonna change then I would look into seeing if I could try and change my plans. I would ask that my boss does not wait until the end to give me late tasks. I want to give these tasks my full attention like they deserve. I would take the feedback and think that they see something that maybe I do not see. I would use it as fuel to better myself and move on. I would go even if they are not going. I would try and make them go but if they are not going I would anyway. It might be fun to make new connections.

I would try to work this out with my colleague. I would see what their vacation plans are and compare them to mine. I would try to compromise and make all parties happy. I would finish the project immediately. I do not believe in letting work wait for another day. I do not want to be in a position where I am scrabbling to finish. I would feel bad and like I have no control. However I am not in a position to argue their perspective. I think I would need to take their criticisms into account and do what I have to do to advance. I would still go. It's important to know the people you work with. This would be a good opportunity to meet and interact with other people in my company.

Since my children are grown and I am more flexible with my schedule, I would change my week of vacation. I would work hard and get the project done in case my boss gives me a last minute task. If the boss does not give a last minute task I would see if the first project could be improved. It would hurt my feelings, but I would not let my feelings show.  I am not the boss and I will follow his feedback. I will still go to the social gathering, even though I would prefer my work colleague to be with me.  I am not afraid to be around people I am not familiar with.

I would talk to my colleague about them changing the dates and if the vacation is important for them. If it isnt I would try to convince them to change but if it is I would change my dates to avoid causing problems for them and my supervisor. I would talk to my boss and ask questions about what work they are going to be giving out over the next few weeks and if there are any important cases that are ongoing. If there doesnt seem to be much going on for the next two weeks i would do my workload as i usually would however if there is likely going to be something coming up I would complete my workload quickly in anticipation of my boss giving me a long task.
 I would talk to my boss and give him all the details about why I disagree with the feedback and how I feel it is wrong. I would try and get as much information from him as to why he thinks the feedback is valid and try to tell him how i disagree. I would try to convince my colleague to go however if they dont I would try to go and meet new people at the gathering who i can connect with,

would change my date for vacation is i would not mind and would benefit myself as I would able to make it as a favor to change and be able to call that favor in next time. I would get the project done as soon as possible and it would leave me more time to prepare for any up coming projects that are asked of me in the last minute would ask for more information as to why the feedback was delivered and to find out what further instructions would of been nice to know about, to receive a positive reaction would not have a problem going to the gathering as it would give me a chance to meet other people and have more connections with people at work to either help or receive more information.

I would approach my colleague and agree to let them have the vacation week if they can compensate me in some way for the trouble.  Otherwise, I'll put my foot down and also demand that I have the same vacation week until we are both stuck working. I would complete all of the tasks I have on hand.  Once done, I would go to the boss and ask what other tasks might be coming in the near future.  I would apply a reasonable deadline, and say that I'm not working beyond a certain time period, so "better get moving" would be the message. I would ask to have a 3rd party view the situation and make a determination.  I would not give up a bonus for something that is objectively false. I would not go to this social gathering.  It's not my thing.  I would think it would look better to stick to what I'm comfortable with, as opposed to going anyway just to look better for the company.  I think it would have the reverse effect, so I too would want to skip the gathering.

I would see who requested first. If I did, I would demand to take vacation. If my colleague did, then I would let him go. I would go to my boss and ask if there is anything I can do to get ahead of the project. I would also organize current tasks to amplify my time later. I would feel bad, obviously. I would also feel mad. I would request to speak to the manager and have someone higher ranking there as well. I would lay out why I feel the feedback is not right and get higher ranking opinion. I would go to network with others. Also, being seen at work functions is important for others to view you as invested.

set down and talk to them and see what kind of compromise we could reach. If one of us does not already have plans that cant change maybe that person can reschedule vacation. I would get a jump start and get all current task done and turned in as soon as possible so that if given more task at the last minute i can jump straight on it and give it my full attention. If it is not far or accurate feedback I would try to set down with my boss to discuss the issues and see if we could reach an understanding. I would also bring in a neutral 3rd party that is a equal level as my boss to keep things fair. I would still go to the gathering even if I am not comfortable going alone. It takes me some time but I would try to meet new people and make some new connections.

I would say I took mine first and stand my ground unless the plans hadn't been paid for. If it's already paid for I would push for going since I requested vacation first. I would try to get the project done as soon as possible as that's ideal in any situation. So whether my boss gives a late, long project, I can still make sure everything is taken care of. I would request to have a conversation and ask them to further clarify. I would understand that sometimes you can make a mistake and not realize it. I would go anyways and leave early if it's awkward. It'd be nice to get to know a few people as well so next time there's more people I'd know.

I would reschedule my vacation to a different week. I think there would be a lot of important cases to work so going on vacation would not be top in my priority list. I would do my best to complete this project as soon as I can. This would free up more time for me to handle any additional tasks that my boss might give me. I would feel bad in this situation. I would express my disagreement with the feedback to my manager but in a polite and respectful manner. I think I would attend the social gathering even without my colleague. It would give me a chance to meet new people and get to learn a thing or two about these people and other general topics.

First come first serve. If it was as the same time, it depends on what my plans were, if they were flexible, I could adjust, if not, then I would not be willing to change either
 Finish up the rest of the workload and ask the boss if there's anything else they are aware of that needs doing so I can get a head start I would be annoyed, I would discuss it with colleagues and, if they agreed with me, escalate it. If they didn't, I'd potentially discuss it with another manager of my managers level for their input Try and convince my colleague to go so I can get to know people. If they don't want to go, I'd likely not go also just to avoid looking like an outcast

I will have to ask who had essentially request time off first and what excatly is the vacation being used for. I would like to reach out to all parties involved in and ask what is dire and what can be done for the essentials of the project. What needs to be done to and what areas need the most attention. The reason I ask is because if there is a lot needed but time is a constraint then there needs to be some way to spare some of the project all around evenly. I would like to have an opportunity to speak with someone from the project management and ask what needed to be done to and what areas need to be worked on or looked at for future references. I would like to attend because I feel that it matters. The impact of being involved in settings as such can be beneficial to everyone involved.

I would confirm that my plans could be changed and if so I would make the adjustment.  If my plans were unmovable then I would have to negotiate with my colleague on why I cannot change my time.  If they do not budge then I would ask my supervisor to make the decision and present the information from both of us.  I would want to work with them but I would not roll over in doing so. I would get the project completed now in anticipation that something else will most likely present itself through my boss.  If nothing presents itself I could still use this time with odds and ends in relation to my job I would consider what was said of me and build a defense on why I did not believe it was valid.  I would be respectful but firm in highlighting why the feedback was not accurate.  I would be upset but I would remain calm. I would encourage them to go but if they don't I would still attend.  I would use it as an opportunity to network and meet new people.  Knowing more people in the organization can only help me in the future.

I would volunteer to change my vacation this time with the agreement that I would be given my preference if the same situation occurred again. I would make sure that I did my task as soon as possible so I could be available if my boss gave me a last minute project,
 I would ask my manager to give me advice on what I could do to improve feedback in the future.  I would make sure I did everything in my power to change the manager's opinion of my work.
